  • Tutorial: Bare Greenhouse

Tutorial: Bare Greenhouse

This tutorial simulates an unheated, unventilated greenhouse and shows the two canonical regimes:

  1. Winter sub-cooling — the indoor temperature drops below the outdoor temperature at night because the cover radiates infrared energy to the cold sky;
  2. Summer overheating — the indoor temperature climbs 15–25 °C above ambient at midday because the floor and walls trap absorbed solar radiation faster than the cover can dissipate it.

Both regimes are easy to reproduce with the same GreenhouseParams once the deep ground boundary condition is set correctly.

Setting T_deep correctly

The model has a constant deep-soil boundary condition at the bottom of the soil column (SoilParams.T_deep). The default soil stack uses 8 exponentially increasing layers reaching ~10 m depth (matching EnergyPlus Foundation:Kiva). This is the single most important parameter for an unheated greenhouse: a wrong value silently turns the soil column into a heat sink or source, biasing the indoor air by several °C.

At ≥ 10 m depth, ground temperature equals the annual mean air temperature (Kusuda & Achenbach 1965, Fossa 2023). This is automatically derived from the TMY/EPW weather file:

using GreenhousesJL

# T_deep = annual mean of T_out from EPW (constant at 10m+ depth)
epw = load_epw("weather/lyon.epw")
T_deep = sum(epw.T_out) / length(epw.T_out) + 273.15  # Lyon: 285.05 K = 11.9°C

The default GreenhouseParams and SavonaParams constructors compute T_deep automatically from the TMY data if not explicitly provided. No seasonal or depth correction is needed — at 10 m the annual wave is fully damped.

Summer sealed scenario (Lyon, 14 000 m²)

using GreenhousesJL
using OrdinaryDiffEqTsit5

epw = load_epw("weather/lyon.epw")

# 60-day warmup window so the soil column reaches its summer pseudo-steady state
tmy = epw_to_tmy(epw; start_month=5, n_days=68)

A = 14000.0
T_deep_K = kusuda_t_deep_from_epw(epw; depth=1.3, day_of_year=182)

p = GreenhouseParams(tmy;
    A         = A,
    h_air     = 3.8,
    Kp        = 0.0,                # no heating
    Q_max     = 0.0,
    T_deep    = T_deep_K,
    A_walls   = 2*(100 + 140)*3.8,  # 1824 m² of wall glazing
)

u0 = initial_state(p; T0 = 15.0+273.15, T_soil0 = T_deep_K - 2)
sol = solve(ODEProblem(greenhouse_rhs!, u0, (0.0, 68*86400.0), p),
            Tsit5(); reltol=1e-6, saveat=60.0, maxiters=20_000_000)

Expected output (last 5 days of the run, July 3–7):

T_air    min  27.9  mean  45.3  max  74.4  °C
T_floor  min  35.5  mean  55.5  max  84.7  °C
T_cover  min  19.2  mean  33.0  max  55.0  °C
ΔT mean  +23.5     ΔT max  +47.4

The greenhouse overheats by +23 °C in the daily mean and reaches +47 °C above outdoor at the afternoon peak. This is the Mediterranean "forcing-house" regime: a sealed structure under full sun without ventilation or shading. The cross-validation against EnergyPlus on this scenario reaches ±2.7 °C in the mean across 9 (size, climate) configurations (see the Validation page).

Winter sub-cooling scenario (Brussels, 14 000 m²)

tmy = load_tmy("Greenhouses/Resources/Data/10Dec-22Nov.txt")

p = GreenhouseParams(tmy;
    A      = 14000.0,
    h_air  = 3.8,
    T_deep = 4.0 + 273.15,    # winter Kusuda value at 1.3 m for Brussels in December
)
u0 = initial_state(p; T0 = 5.0 + 273.15, T_soil0 = 4.0 + 273.15)

sol = solve(ODEProblem(greenhouse_rhs!, u0, (0.0, 7*86400.0), p),
            Tsit5(); reltol=1e-6, saveat=3600.0)

Analyse the depression:

using Statistics
T_air = sol[2, :] .- 273.15
T_out = [interp_tmy(p.tmy, t)[1] for t in sol.t]
depression = T_air .- T_out

println("Mean T_air:       $(round(mean(T_air), digits=1)) °C")
println("Mean T_out:       $(round(mean(T_out), digits=1)) °C")
println("Mean depression:  $(round(mean(depression), digits=1)) °C")

Expected:

Mean T_air:       5.4 °C
Mean T_out:       8.7 °C
Mean depression:  -3.3 °C

The greenhouse sits 3.3 °C below the outdoor air on average — the classical "frost in a glass box" effect that catches first-time growers off guard.

Physical interpretation

In winter:

  1. The glass cover ($\varepsilon = 0.84$) radiates infrared energy to the sky ($T_{sky} \approx -3$°C in December), losing approximately 28 W/m² on average.
  2. The outdoor air partially compensates by warming the cover via convection (≈ 30 W/m²), but the net balance leaves a depression of −3.3 °C below ambient.
  3. During daytime, solar transmission through the glass briefly warms the interior above ambient.

In summer:

  1. The cover transmits ~89 % of the incoming solar radiation. With dark concrete ($\rho_{flr} = 0.07$), nearly 74 % of the incoming irradiance ends up absorbed at the floor after multi-bounce.
  2. The convective and radiative loss paths (cover → sky, walls → ground, wall → outside) are not strong enough to compete with peak solar (~700 W/m² at noon).
  3. The result is a 15–25 °C overheating in the mean and 35–47 °C at the peak. Without ventilation or thermal screens this scenario is non-survivable for crops — the Ventilation tutorial shows how to bring the temperature back down.

References

  • Kusuda, T. & Achenbach, P.R. (1965). Earth temperature and thermal diffusivity at selected stations in the United States. ASHRAE Transactions, 71(1), 61–75.
  • Vanthoor, B.H.E. (2011). A model-based greenhouse design method. PhD thesis, Wageningen University.
  • de Zwart, H.F. (1996). Analyzing energy-saving options in greenhouse cultivation using a simulation model. PhD thesis, Wageningen University.
